TRANSCRIPT
This section is all about how to scale. Again, scaling is this idea where your
business can grow to a large degree. Maybe it can double or it can quadruple or it could
10x or it could 100x or 1000x. Some business will scale like that, some won't, but this is
how to scale your business. The first thing I want to do is to have a caveat that sometimes
you have to do things that don't scale, and specially at the start of your business, it's just
that's the way to get started. When I first started out, I would personally answer every
email that came and every question that came in, and I would invite those questions.
Once you get a very large presence, large email list, large social following, at some point
you can't do that anymore or else it will just burn you out.
But in the beginning, I did it, and I created a lot of relationships with a lot of
people, got lots of word of mouth by doing it. I know someone that when he first started,
everyone who joined his email list, after a few emails, he would send them an email
inviting them to a personal one-on-one phone call, a short phone call. Then, he would get
on the phone call, he would get to know them, this was a way for him to find about his
market, about his avatar. Then, he would offer them some type of a coaching program, so
he could make sales. It was lucrative, he converted a lot of people. That would inherently
be something that doesn't scale.
If you’re getting on the phone with everyone who joins your email list and selling to
some of them, it's a great way to generate some early sales, a great way to generate word
of mouth, positioning. Understanding scale, lots of great things that come out of that, but
it doesn't scale - it doesn't scale. But there're a lot of powerful things. Sometimes you
have to do things that don't scale and understand that, and understand that sooner or
later you'll stop those things.
There are some things that might not seem to scale, but they end up scaling. Like,
for instance, I send a hand-written postcard out to everyone who purchases this Product
Launch Formula from me. In the early days, I wrote out that postcard to every single
person for the first, I don't know, maybe probably 1000 people that bought. Then, they
became printed postcards that I signed and that was probably the next couple thousand
people.
Eventually, to print the card with my printed signature that's hand addressed by
people that I employ. That will still get a personal touch out there, but I'm no longer
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

involved in it and it does scale.
Sometimes you start off with things that you’re doing to create an impact, to create
the impression you want, the positioning you want, the service level you want, and even
though they don't scale, you can find a way to scale them eventually.
Moving on, one the things, this is a key one, this is the idea that your strength
eventually becomes your weakness. That might sound a little weird but let me explain.
For me, one of the things that I'm strong in - is marketing. I'm really good at putting
together marketing campaigns, putting together launches, putting together sales
processes - that's just something that just comes naturally to me, I'm one of the very best
in the world - when it comes to launches I'm probably the best in the world. It's a huge
strength. But then, as the business grows and we start to grow the team, and we start to
hire people, it's hard to hire people that are as good, or better than me, when I'm, if not
the best in the world, one of the very best in the world.
I don't want to hire someone for that, I don't want to hand it over to someone
because I'm so good at it, and comes so naturally to me, it just seems sort of crazy. But
then eventually, the business grows to the level where I become a bottleneck. Because
even though I'm really great at it, there's just me, one person, and I become the
bottleneck. That goes from being the strength to being the weakness. If it's a strength and
I’m doing one campaign every other month or several times a year, but all of a sudden the
business has grown to the point where we need a new campaign every two weeks and I
can't do that, I become the bottleneck. I become the choke point. I constrain what the
team can do. It's hard for me to hire because I'm so good at it and it's hard to find
someone who can perform at my level.
You have this - that your strength becomes your weakness. You have to try to get
ahead of it, you have to try to hire for it. It's not an easy thing, but it's something you
have to do. As you scale up, this goes for leverage as well, but as you scale up, at some
point, one of the most important, if not the most important things that you do, or your
team does, is recruit - find great people, because as you scale you can't do it by yourself.
Nothing scales if it's just you, so you start to grow your team. This is a mistake I
personally made - I took way too long to start growing my team. But once you start doing
that, you realize getting great people on your team becomes one of the most important
aspects of you and your team.
By the way, we're always looking for amazing people on my team, we've got a

fantastic team, an incredible culture, we get a ton a done, we have a lot of fun and if you're

Now in scaling - scaling with my team… it's one of the big shifts in the last few years
for me. It's probably been about three years or so where we really started growing. It's
also been one of the big shifts in my industry. People are really starting to grow teams.
Like I said earlier, I was slow to do this, I got some really bad advice from a guru a long
ago, 15 years ago or more ago, probably near 20 years ago. "Don't hire no matter what -
it's going to make your life a living hell. People are horrible, they're hard to work with,
they're just going to disappoint you and on and on and on, don't do it, don't hire, avoid it
at all costs." So I did that for a very long time, I built up a very large business before we
had this almost skeleton team, hardly anyone on the team.
But a few years ago, I just realized how bad that advice was and so I started
growing my team. It takes effort, it's not an easy thing to do, I had to learn to be a leader
for our team, I had to learn to grow leadership within the team. We had to learn how to
forge the culture we want, how to create the culture we want, how to decide how we're
going to interact with each other, who we were going to be as an organization, what we
were going to value, how we were going to communicate with each other, how we were
going to run projects, there's a lot of work there, it's not easy.
But it is super rewarding. Because you get, at least for me, I get to work with
amazing people, people that just put their heart and soul into the business and that just
show up and just kick ass and do a great job. It's been wonderful, hard but wonderful, and
it allows you to scale. In fact, this is the only way you're really going to grow. You just
absolutely… you can't do it all alone, you just can't. If you try to do it all alone or with a
minimal team, then you're not going to be working within your unique ability, you're
going to be doing things that you might not be good at or you might be doing things
you're good at but not great at, you might even be doing things that you're excellent at but

it's not your genius zone. Eventually, you should even be handing over those things that

Scaling with teams, critical. There's just no way you can scale without a team.
Again, I'm just plugging myself again - if you are interested in joining my team,
jeffwalker.com/careers, we're always interested in amazing people.
Another thing is scaling your ad spend, or if you're starting to spend on advertising
if you're not doing so. A lot of times people, when they start up, they just worry about
driving traffic via social media, like free social media, or free traffic, which is great, a great
way to get started if you can pull it off, it's getting harder and harder to do, but at the end
of the day, you have to understand that free traffic has a cost, it always has a cost and it's
time, it's time and education. It generally doesn't scale.
When you can start to spend on your advertising, and have that advertising, have
the results, the ROAI, the Return on Advertising Investment, payoff, then you can start to
scale with your ads, you could scale your ad spend. That is a really, it's a big switch, when
you can flip that switch - where you're making money with your advertising, then you can
start to grow your advertising, that gives you some real freedom. Now, one the things that
you're going to be focusing at is your cost of acquisition… how much does it cost to
acquire a customer? That will become one of the key driving numbers in your business.
One thing to remember is that as you scale your advertising, your cost of
acquisition will almost, surely go up. If early it's costing you $5 to get a lead, and $20 to
make a sale, but then if you grow your traffic by 10 times, it might have cost you $20 to
get a lead and $100 to make a sale because it's just, initially, you're going to be soaking up
the low-hanging fruit, you're going to be gathering the low-hanging fruit, and after that's
gone, then it gets harder and harder to find a universe, a bigger universe, that is a good
match for your service or your product. Your cost acquisition will go up, that's just a fact
of life -be ready for it.
That being said, if you're going about how to make money (be profitable) from paid
advertising, it’s a huge switch that you want to flip that will allow you to scale.
Something else that's involved in scaling and leverage is your market and how
quickly your market is refreshing, or how quickly it’s turning. For instance, if you're
selling training to real estate agents…, something I forget exactly what the number is, but
it's shockingly high how many new realtors come into the market every year and how
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

many leave the market. I'm going to make a wild guess, this could be completely off, but I
think something like 20% or maybe 25% of the people turn it over every year. If there's,
say 100,000 realtors, that means that 20,000 of them, in any given year, will leave the
market and 20,000 new ones will replace them. Again, I've heard these numbers, but
that's been a long time, so I'm sure I'm completely way off in these numbers, but the point
is that there's a huge amount of churn in that market, and it continually refreshes.
If you're selling investing for real estate agents, that market just continually churns,
and it refreshes, and it gives you new prospects all the time. Now that also makes
retention - like if you have some kind of continuity - it makes retaining people as clients
probably a little harder. Because, yes, some of them will stick, but if 20% are turning over
every single year, and that means a lot of them are going to be turning over in their
second years and third year as well. If 80% lasts the first year, maybe only 70% lasts the
second year, maybe only 60% lasts a third year and you're trying to sell continuity, a lot of
those people are going to be leaving, not because of your program, or your training, or
your service, but because they are leaving the market.
Different markets have different characteristics, that membership program, that
mutual fund software program I mentioned earlier on that I sold, that was for the users of
this certain investment software. 	There was no turn at all in that, people loved it, they
stuck with it, they stuck with me year after year after year but the market wasn't
refreshing. That was like a dying piece of software. In fact, it was a DOS software, if any
of you know what that means, it predated Windows, it didn't even really work very well in
Windows. They weren't updating it; they weren't bringing in new clients. That market was
not refreshing. It didn't churn, but it was not refreshing.
I say this because different markets are different, and they all have different
characteristics. I have a couple of clients, Des and Michael, they're in Ireland, they sell to
small accounting firms in Ireland. I think they said there's something like 2,000 of those
firms, somewhere around that range. Not much churn, those businesses tend to stay in
business, they're small accounting firms, with a small number of clients, so they don't
churn, those clients stick around. But there aren't a lot of new people coming, new
businesses starting in that market. So, it's got low churn, and super low refresh. Again,
different markets are different.
It doesn't make it good or bad, it's just part of the whole picture when you're
thinking about leveraging and scaling.

One great way to scale is to add in another offer or another product. My friend
Eben Pagan, he got his start teaching men about dating - how to get dates. I remember
him telling me "You know, this is a really nice business, it was growing," but then when he
added a second product, he doubled his business almost overnight. Because all of a
sudden now he had two products to sell, he had a second product to sell to people who
had already bought from them, plus he had a second product to act as a front end. And
he'd literally doubled his business with that second offer.
Think about, this is something I've seen work many times, I've done it in my
business, where I've added in, for instance, I've got my $2000 Product Launch Formula,
but as of right now, I have this product called Launch Club, which is currently priced at
$12000. All of a sudden, I started to ... I don't know if I was doubling my business, but it
added a huge chunk of revenue to my business and it allowed us to serve a whole another
group of people in a deeper way, in a stronger way.
We added another offer, in that case, you could call that an upsell, although it
wasn't technically an upsell, or we didn’t make that upsell immediately, it was generally
that offer was made months after people go through Product Launch Formula. So there
could be upsells or there could be sequential offers, there could be cross-sells, but add in
another product, like adding continuity as your second product, or your third product.
Another thing, you can add in another sales process. If you're using launches,
maybe add a webinar. If you're using webinars, add in launches, or add in a second
launch sequence, a second webinar sequence. So someone comes through the sequence,
then maybe after you make the offer, then you deliver value, value, value for a little bit,
then you come back and you run a second sequence. It could be a webinar; it could be a
product launch. Then you come back and you offer another sequence after that. They
don't have to be super long, super involved sequences, but give people another chance to
buy - either that product or another product.
One of the things that comes up a lot ... This isn't technically how you scale or how
to get leverage, but as you grow your business you need to think about risk. Because every
business has risk points. They could be hard to see. Like if you look at Kodak, the
dominance of Kodak in the film industry, in the photography industry, is hard to
overstate, they just completely dominated it. Then, when digital photography came
around, they were very slow to jump on board and they're gone now, the company is gone.
They went from being the absolute dominant player in a huge industry to non-existence
in, I don't know, a decade, two decades max.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. 	I 	www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
Risk points can be hard to see. Blockbuster, huge retail chain that rented DVDs,
and I guess before that VHS cassettes, they're gone, Netflix took them out in a very short
period of time, they are just gone.
When I think about risk, one of the things I like to look at in my business, one of
the ways that I get at the idea of where are my risk points, is I periodically take some time,
take a little retreat, and I say, sit down and I say "If I was looking to buy a business, and I
came across my current business, I didn't know them, but I just came across, you know,
in a parallel universe, I've came across myself trying to sell my business, why would I
object to buying it? Why would I not want to buy it? What would give me pause to say 'I
don't like this thing about this business'? This is the thing that scares me about this
business."
Every business has risk points, every business has weaknesses, every business, and
your business is no different, and my business is no different. I look at "Why wouldn't I
buy this business?" And then, of course once I identify the weak points, then I do my best
to mitigate those weak points, those risk points. That's an exercise I highly suggest you
do.
Okay. The next section we're going to go into the advanced stuff, really, this is
about leverage, and scale, and creating the business that you want. More importantly, the
life that you want.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
